skill.name: ghostty
skill.description: Control the Ghostty terminal emulator with documented CLI actions for windows, tabs, splits, fonts, and configuration.
skill.summary: Use Ghostty CLI actions to manage terminal windows, tabs, splits, fonts, themes, and configuration.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution patterns, but they are Markdown examples for the Ghostty CLI rather than embedded executable code. The skill intentionally guides agents to run local terminal-control commands, including launching commands with Ghostty, so it carries medium operational risk but no confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Terminal Control Through External Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to use Ghostty CLI actions that create windows, tabs, splits, close the current surface, reload configuration, and launch a command with ghostty -e. These commands are legitimate for this skill, but they can alter a user's active terminal session or start local processes if executed without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly document Ghostty commands that control terminal windows and can launch a local command. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is operational misuse rather than malware.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across many lines. The reviewed file contains Markdown inline code and fenced bash examples, with no Ruby code, no backtick command substitution, and no script that executes automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is plain Markdown documentation and the flagged syntax appears inside inline code or fenced command examples. No executable Ruby backtick pattern was found in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Ghostty Config Path Is a Normal User Configuration Location
low_findings.description: The hidden file finding points to the documented Ghostty config path at ~/.config/ghostty/config. This is a standard per-user configuration location and the skill does not include instructions to read secrets or exfiltrate file contents.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only hidden path evidence is the documented Ghostty configuration file location. No broader filesystem traversal, secret harvesting, or network transmission is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Is Unsupported
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the description line. No cryptographic operation, hash function, encryption routine, or password handling appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is the skill description and only mentions controlling Ghostty. There is no evidence of cryptographic code or weak cryptographic usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Launch Option
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented ghostty -e option starts Ghostty with a command. This is useful terminal behavior, but assistants should only use it with explicit user intent because it starts a local process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command launch example is explicit and directly tied to the terminal emulator. It is not malicious by itself, but it is the highest-impact command pattern in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Session-Altering IPC Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented Ghostty IPC actions can create, navigate, close, and reconfigure terminal surfaces. These actions should be treated as user-visible state changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines list concrete Ghostty actions that affect the running terminal session. The commands are legitimate but can disrupt a user's workspace if used unexpectedly.

content.value_statement: Terminal layout and configuration changes can interrupt focused development when done manually. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise Ghostty command reference for controlled terminal actions.
actual_capabilities: Lists Ghostty CLI locations for direct execution or symlinked use.
Documents actions for creating windows, tabs, and terminal splits.
Shows commands for navigating splits and closing the current surface.
Provides font size, theme, font, keybind, and configuration inspection commands.
Documents launch options for config files, working directories, and commands.
Includes version and configuration validation commands for troubleshooting.
limitations: Requires Ghostty to be installed and available on the target machine.
Commands are focused on Ghostty and do not manage other terminal emulators.
The skill is a command reference and does not validate user configuration content.
Some commands can change the active terminal session and should be confirmed first.

use_cases.title: Set Up Development Layouts
use_cases.description: Create Ghostty windows, tabs, or splits before starting a multi-service development session.

use_cases.title: Inspect Terminal Configuration
use_cases.description: List themes, fonts, keybinds, and active configuration values while tuning a Ghostty setup.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Ghostty Behavior
use_cases.description: Check the installed Ghostty version and validate configuration changes before relying on them.

prompt_templates.title: List Ghostty Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Ghostty command to list available actions, but do not run it yet.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Split Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare Ghostty commands to create one right split and one down split for my current terminal session.

prompt_templates.title: Review Configuration Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me inspect my Ghostty configuration, available themes, fonts, and keybinds using safe read-only commands.

prompt_templates.title: Launch a Project Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan Ghostty commands to start a terminal in my project directory with a specific config file. Ask before running any command.

output_examples.input: I need a new Ghostty tab and then a right split.
output_examples.output: Use the new tab action first, then create a right split in that tab. I would ask for confirmation before changing your active terminal layout.

output_examples.input: How can I check whether my Ghostty config is valid?
output_examples.output: Run the Ghostty configuration validation action. It checks the config without requiring manual inspection of every setting.

output_examples.input: Show me what Ghostty themes are available.
output_examples.output: Use the Ghostty theme listing action. It returns the installed themes that Ghostty can use.

best_practices: Confirm before running commands that create, close, or rearrange terminal surfaces.
Use read-only listing commands before changing configuration or layout.
Validate Ghostty configuration after edits and before reloading it.
anti_patterns: Do not run ghostty -e with a command the user did not explicitly request.
Do not close terminal surfaces without confirming the current context.
Do not assume the Ghostty binary path is available on non-macOS systems.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI tools reference Ghostty CLI commands for terminal windows, tabs, splits, configuration, fonts, themes, and debugging.

faq.question: Does this skill install Ghostty?
faq.answer: No. Ghostty must already be installed and available through the documented app path or a ghostty command.

faq.question: Can it change my active terminal session?
faq.answer: Yes. Some commands can create, close, or rearrange Ghostty surfaces, so confirmation is recommended.

faq.question: Does it read private files?
faq.answer: No evidence found. It only documents the standard Ghostty configuration path and related CLI inspection commands.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides local terminal commands that can change session state or start processes.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
